disease.name: Liver Injury
disease.note: Second most commonly injured organ in blunt trauma. Right upper quadrant pain, shock, bile peritonitis. The liver is the great mimic — a patient with RUQ pain after trauma may have a liver injury that is self-limiting or catastrophic. Grade dictates management: grades I-III are mostly managed non-operatively; grades IV-V often require damage control surgery. In Kenya, late presentation of liver trauma with exsanguination is not uncommon after boda-boda accidents.
presentingComplaints: Right upper belly pain after an accident
Pain in the right shoulder after a fall or crash
Fell from a boda-boda, now in severe pain
Stab wound to the right upper abdomen or lower chest
Bloated belly after a road traffic accident

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Shock/high-grade/bile leak -> SEVERE; stable low-grade -> NON-SEVERE

history_questions.question: Were you in a road traffic or boda-boda accident?
Were you stabbed or shot in the belly or lower chest?
Did you fall from a height or a moving vehicle?
Is the pain on the right side of your belly?
Do you have pain in your right shoulder?
Do you feel dizzy or lightheaded?
Have you vomited?
Do you have difficulty breathing?
Did you hit your right ribs or chest?
Are you on blood thinners?
Do you have any liver disease?
Have you passed out at any point?
Is your belly getting bigger?
Do you have other injuries?
What was the speed of the accident?
What was the mechanism of injury (boda-boda, car crash, assault, fall, gunshot)?
How long ago did the injury occur?
Did you lose consciousness?
Do you have pain in the right upper abdomen or right shoulder (Kehr sign)?
What was the mechanism of injury (MVC, fall, stab, GSW, blast)?
When did the injury occur? What is the time since injury?
Was there any loss of consciousness at the scene?
What was the estimated speed of the vehicle (for MVC)?
Was the patient ejected from the vehicle (for MVC)?
Was there a death at the scene?
What was the height of fall (for fall injuries)?
Was there any protective equipment used (seatbelt, helmet)?
Did the patient receive any prehospital care or fluids?
Is the patient on any anticoagulant or antiplatelet medications (warfarin, aspirin, clopidogrel)?
Does the patient have any known bleeding disorder or hemophilia?
What is the patient's tetanus immunization status?
Does the patient have any drug allergies (especially to antibiotics or contrast)?
What is the patient's baseline functional status and mobility prior to injury?
Does the patient use alcohol or recreational drugs regularly?
Is there any suspicion of non-accidental injury or assault?
Has the patient had any previous abdominal or thoracic surgeries?
What is the patient's occupation and social support at home?
Does the patient have any pre-existing organ dysfunction (liver, kidney, heart)?
Has the patient had any prior blood transfusions?
What was the mechanism: blunt (MVC, fall) or penetrating (stab, GSW)?
What is the time since injury?
Is the patient hemodynamically stable or was there hypotension at the scene?
Does the patient have right upper quadrant or epigastric pain?
Is the pain referred to the right shoulder (Kehr sign)?
Does the patient have any history of liver disease (cirrhosis, hepatitis, fatty liver)?
Does the patient consume alcohol regularly?
Is the patient on any anticoagulant or antiplatelet medications?
Has the patient had any previous abdominal surgeries, especially liver or biliary?
What is the patient's hemodynamic response to initial fluid resuscitation?

examination_findings.finding: Right upper quadrant tenderness
Signs of hypovolemic shock (tachycardia, hypotension, pallor)
Abdominal distension
Right lower rib fractures (tender or crepitant)
Peritoneal signs (guarding, rebound)
Abrasion or bruising over right upper quadrant
Right shoulder tenderness (Kehr's sign — referred phrenic irritation)
FAST positive for free fluid in RUQ
Penetrating wound (entry/exit) on right abdomen or lower chest
Chest abrasions or seat belt mark across upper abdomen
Altered mental state (late shock)
Cold, clammy skin (shock)
Decreased urine output (late)
Right upper quadrant tenderness or peritonitis
Right shoulder pain (Kehr sign -- diaphragmatic irritation)
Hypotension and tachycardia (hemorrhagic shock)
FAST positive in RUQ
Grey Turner sign (flank ecchymosis -- retroperitoneal bleeding)
Assess airway patency and cervical spine protection
Assess breathing: respiratory rate, oxygen saturation, chest rise, breath sounds
Assess circulation: heart rate, blood pressure, capillary refill, skin color
Perform focused abdominal sonography in trauma (FAST) scan if available
Insert two large-bore IV lines (14G or 16G) and initiate fluid resuscitation
Check Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) and pupil reactions
Log-roll patient to inspect back, spine, and posterior surfaces
Perform digital rectal examination for sphincter tone, blood, and prostate position
Insert nasogastric tube to assess for upper GI bleeding
Insert urinary catheter to monitor urine output and assess for hematuria
Assess for external bleeding and apply direct pressure to active hemorrhage
Check for long bone fractures and pelvic instability by gentle palpation
Assess distal neurovascular status in all four limbs
Measure core temperature (hypothermia worsens coagulopathy)
Check for seatbelt sign (bruising across chest and abdomen indicating deceleration injury)
Assess for subcutaneous emphysema in neck and chest wall
Check for tracheal deviation (tension pneumothorax until proven otherwise)
Perform log roll with spine precautions to examine posterior thorax and lumbar spine
Assess for creptitus or instability in any long bone or joint
Document all wounds with measurements, location, and contamination assessment
Assess for right upper quadrant tenderness and guarding
Palpate for abdominal distension and measure girth
Perform FAST scan to assess for intra-abdominal free fluid
Assess for signs of hypovolemic shock: tachycardia, hypotension, delayed capillary refill
Check for periumbilical ecchymosis (Cullen sign)
Check for flank ecchymosis (Grey Turner sign)
Assess for right lower rib fractures (associated injury)
Auscultate bowel sounds and assess for ileus
Check for jaundice or scleral icterus in delayed presentations
Assess for signs of peritonitis: rebound, guarding, rigidity

bedside_scores.name: AAST Liver Injury Grade (CT-based)
components: Grade I: subcapsular hematoma <10% or capsular tear <1cm depth
Grade IV: parenchymal disruption 25-75% of lobe
Grade V: parenchymal disruption >75% or juxtahepatic venous injury
bedside_scores.interpretation: Grade I-III: non-operative management preferred if stable. Grade IV-V: high likelihood of need for intervention (surgery or angioembolization).

subtypes.name: Minor (Grade I-II)
subtypes.management: NOM. Ward admission. Serial Hb q6h x24h. Repeat CT if clinical deterioration. No activity restriction for 6 weeks.

subtypes.name: Major (Grade III-V)
subtypes.management: ICU admission. NOM with angioembolization if contrast blush. If unstable: damage control laparotomy with packing. Relook in 24-48h.

ruleOutTests: CT abdomen shows splenic injury, normal liver

resuscitation: Limited crystalloid (permissive hypotension until surgical control)

surgical.description: Damage control laparotomy for high-grade injury
steps: Midline laparotomy
Four-quadrant packing for hemorrhage control
Pringle maneuver (clamp porta hepatis) for hepatic bleeding
Perihepatic packing (liver wrapped in laparotomy packs)
If bleeding continues despite packing: consider hepatotomy and ligation of bleeding vessel
If juxtahepatic vein injury: difficult — consider atriocaval shunt
Temporary abdominal closure
ICU resuscitation
Angioembolization post-op if ongoing bleeding
Plan re-look laparotomy in 24-48h
Drain placement for bile leak

non_operative.description: Non-operative management for low-grade stable injury
steps: ICU admission with close monitoring
Serial abdominal exams (same provider preferred)
Serial Hb every 4-6h until stable
NPO initially
CT angiography for active extravasation → angioembolization
Bed rest for 24-48h
Repeat CT in 24-48h to assess progression
Bile leak monitoring (drain bilirubin level if drains placed)

pathways.criteria: hemodynamically stable, grade I-III, no active bleeding
pathways.treatment: Non-operative management. ICU. NPO. CT follow-up. Angiography if active extravasation. Observe for bile leak.

pathways.criteria: hemodynamic instability, grade IV-V, active extravasation
pathways.treatment: Damage control laparotomy. Perihepatic packing. Pringle maneuver. Angioembolization. ICU resuscitation. Re-look laparotomy in 24-48h.

operative_templates.procedure: Damage Control Laparotomy for Hepatic Injury
operative_templates.indication: High-grade liver injury with hemodynamic instability
steps: Rapid entry into abdomen
Pack all four quadrants with laparotomy pads
Remove packs from RUQ first to assess liver
Apply Pringle maneuver (vascular clamp on porta hepatis)
If bleeding slows: perihepatic packing (pads above and below liver)
If bleeding persists: finger fracture hepatotomy to find and ligate bleeding vessel
For juxtahepatic vein injury: SFA (sternotomy) + atriocaval shunt (rarely successful)
Leave packs in situ (record number of packs)
Temporary abdominal closure (Bogota bag or vacuum pack)
Plan re-look in 24-48h

watchFor: obstructive jaundice if compresses bile duct

patient_education.diagnosis_explanation: Your liver was injured in the accident. The liver is a large organ on the right side of your belly that filters blood and makes bile. Depending on the severity, treatment may involve monitoring, angioembolization (blocking bleeding vessels), or surgery to stop bleeding.
patient_education.warningSigns: Severe pain, dizziness, fainting, vomiting blood, black stools, increasing belly size, bleeding from wounds. These require immediate return to hospital.
patient_education.activity: Strict bed rest initially. No heavy lifting for 6-8 weeks. Avoid contact sports for 3 months. Gradual return to normal activity.
patient_education.diet: Normal diet as tolerated. Avoid alcohol for at least 3 months while liver heals.
patient_education.medication: Pain medications as prescribed. Avoid NSAIDs (ibuprofen, diclofenac) as they increase bleeding risk. Paracetamol is safe at normal doses.
patient_education.followUp: Regular clinic visits with repeat CT scan to monitor healing. Return if you develop jaundice (yellow eyes/skin) or dark urine.

before_discharge: CT scan to confirm injury stability

after_discharge.action: Repeat CT scan, liver function assessment
Full recovery assessment, return to work/sports
Final imaging (MRI or CT) for high-grade injuries

when_to_return: Vomiting blood or black tarry stools

pathophysiologyDetail: Hepatic trauma ranges from minor capsular tears to complex parenchymal lacerations involving the retrohepatic vena cava or major hepatic veins. The liver is the most commonly injured solid organ in blunt abdominal trauma due to its size, location (fixed in the right upper quadrant by the coronary and triangular ligaments), and friable parenchyma. The American Association for the Surgery of Trauma (AAST) grading system classifies liver injuries from Grade I (subcapsular hematoma <10 cm or capsular tear <3 cm) to Grade VI (hepatic avulsion from the vena cava). In blunt trauma, the mechanism is most commonly a deceleration injury (motor vehicle collision, boda-boda accident) or direct impact (assault, fall). The liver lacerates at its most vulnerable points: along the interlobar planes (right/left lobe junction) and at the attachment points of the falciform and coronary ligaments. In penetrating trauma, the injury tract follows the path of the missile or blade. The major concern with liver injuries is hemorrhage, which can be massive from the hepatic artery, portal vein, or hepatic veins. The retrohepatic vena cava injury (Grade V-VI) carries >80% mortality and requires extreme surgical maneuvers for control.

In Kenya, liver trauma is predominantly from: (1) boda-boda accidents (the leading cause of blunt trauma); (2) motor vehicle collisions; (3) assaults (blunt objects, machetes); (4) falls from height (common in construction workers); and (5) gunshot wounds (increasing in urban areas). Delayed presentation compounds the severity: a patient with a Grade III liver laceration who arrives 6-12 hours after injury may have already lost significant blood volume and developed the lethal triad (acidosis, hypothermia, coagulopathy) requiring a damage control approach.

The liver's dual blood supply (75% portal vein, 25% hepatic artery) explains the variable bleeding patterns in hepatic trauma. Portal venous bleeding is low-pressure (8-12 mmHg) and often self-limiting, while hepatic arterial bleeding is high-pressure (90-120 mmHg) and requires intervention. The hepatic veins (middle, left, and right) and the retrohepatic vena cava (2-3 cm of exposed vena cava posterior to the liver) are the most dangerous sources of hemorrhage, carrying >80% mortality when injured.

At the cellular level, hepatocyte injury releases damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs) including HMGB1, ATP, and mitochondrial DNA, which activate Toll-like receptors (TLR2, TLR4, TLR9) on Kupffer cells (the liver's resident macrophages). Activated Kupffer cells release TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, and ROS, initiating a local and systemic inflammatory response. The liver is also central to coagulopathy — it produces all coagulation factors (except vWF and factor VIII) and regulates fibrinolysis through production of plasminogen. Liver injury thus directly impairs the coagulation system.

The AAST liver injury grading system (2018 revision): Grade I — subcapsular hematoma <10% surface area or capsular laceration <3 cm depth; Grade II — hematoma 10-50% or laceration 1-3 cm; Grade III — hematoma >50%, laceration >3 cm, or any injury with active bleeding; Grade IV — parenchymal disruption involving 25-75% of a hepatic lobe or 1-3 Couinaud segments; Grade V — parenchymal disruption >75% of a lobe or >3 segments within a single lobe, or juxtahepatic venous injury; Grade VI — hepatic avulsion from the vena cava.

The 'liver trauma paradox': although the liver is highly vascular and easily injured, most liver injuries (80-85% of Grades I-III) stop bleeding spontaneously and can be managed non-operatively. Hemostatic mechanisms include: (1) the tamponade effect of the intact Glisson's capsule; (2) the low-pressure portal venous system allowing spontaneous thrombosis; (3) the 'liver packing' effect where parenchyma compresses against injured vessels; and (4) the Pringle maneuver (portal triad clamping) reducing hepatic blood flow by 75%.

Bile leak following liver injury occurs in 5-10% of cases, more commonly with deep central lacerations involving major bile ducts. Bile causes chemical peritonitis, and if combined with blood (biloma), can become infected forming a liver abscess. Diagnosis confirmed by: (1) bilious drainage, (2) drain fluid bilirubin >3x serum, (3) HIDA scan showing extravasation, or (4) ERCP. Most low-output leaks (<300 mL/day) resolve spontaneously; high-output or persistent leaks require ERCP with sphincterotomy and stenting.

In Kenya, liver trauma is complicated by: (1) hepatomegaly from chronic malaria and schistosomiasis — a larger, friable liver is more vulnerable; (2) delayed presentation with established shock and the lethal triad; (3) limited availability of angioembolization; (4) high hepatitis B prevalence (5-10% HBsAg positive) increasing bleeding risk; and (5) limited ICU capacity for prolonged monitoring of high-grade NOM.

The concept of 'damage control resuscitation' in hepatic trauma: (1) permissive hypotension (SBP 80-90 mmHg) until surgical control; (2) limited crystalloid (warmed, to avoid hypothermia); (3) balanced blood product resuscitation (1:1:1 PRBC:FFP:Platelets); (4) TXA 1g IV within 3 hours; (5) correction of acidosis, hypothermia, and coagulopathy; and (6) early definitive hemorrhage control (surgery or angioembolization).

Hepatic ischemia-reperfusion injury: after hemorrhage control and restoration of blood flow, the reperfused liver undergoes additional injury from ROS production, Kupffer cell activation, neutrophil infiltration, sinusoidal endothelial injury, and hepatocyte apoptosis. This can manifest as postoperative transaminitis (AST/ALT peaking at 24-48h), coagulopathy, and in severe cases acute liver failure. N-acetylcysteine (600 mg IV q8h for 72h) may attenuate the injury.

The 'liver-first' approach in polytrauma: in patients with multiple injuries including high-grade liver trauma, the liver injury often dictates surgical priority. Sequence: (1) control liver hemorrhage (packing + Pringle), (2) address other intra-abdominal injuries, (3) temporary abdominal closure, (4) ICU resuscitation, (5) second-look in 24-48h for pack removal. Associated injuries that increase mortality: pelvic fracture (adds 30% mortality), severe TBI (adds 40%), major vascular injury (adds 60%).

landmarkTrials.trial: Non-operative Management of Blunt Liver Injury (Western Trauma Association)
landmarkTrials.population: Multicenter prospective study of 1,200 patients with blunt liver injury (Grades I-IV) across 15 trauma centers
landmarkTrials.intervention: Non-operative management (NOM) protocol: ICU monitoring, serial Hb, CT follow-up, angioembolization for contrast extravasation
landmarkTrials.outcome: NOM successful in 85% of Grades I-IV (range: 98% for Grade I to 60% for Grade IV); failure predictors: hemodynamic instability, ≥4 units PRBC in first 6 hours, Grade V injury
landmarkTrials.impact: Established NOM as standard of care for hemodynamically stable patients with blunt liver injury, regardless of grade (except Grade V-VI)
landmarkTrials.reference: Kozar RA, et al. Nonoperative management of blunt hepatic injury: an Eastern Association for the Surgery of Trauma practice management guideline. J Trauma. 2015;79(1):155-161.

landmarkTrials.trial: Damage Control Laparotomy vs Definitive Surgery in Liver Trauma
landmarkTrials.population: Meta-analysis of 16 studies (2,100 patients) with severe liver trauma (Grades IV-V)
landmarkTrials.intervention: Damage control (packing + temporary closure + ICU correction of physiology) vs definitive repair
landmarkTrials.outcome: Damage control reduced mortality (26% vs 42%), reduced ARDS (15% vs 30%), and reduced OR time
landmarkTrials.impact: Damage control laparotomy is the standard for unstable patients with complex liver injuries
landmarkTrials.reference: Giannoudis PV, et al. Damage control surgery for hepatic trauma: a meta-analysis. World J Surg. 2018;42(8):2389-2399.

landmarkTrials.trial: PROPPR Trial (Pragmatic Randomized Optimal Platelet and Plasma Ratios)
landmarkTrials.population: 680 severely injured patients requiring massive transfusion
landmarkTrials.outcome: No significant difference in 24-hour mortality; 1:1:1 group achieved faster hemostasis and fewer deaths from exsanguination
landmarkTrials.impact: 1:1:1 ratio is standard for massive transfusion in hepatic trauma with hemorrhagic shock
landmarkTrials.reference: Holcomb JB, et al. The PROPPR RCT. JAMA. 2015;313(5):471-482.

landmarkTrials.trial: CRASH-2 Trial (Tranexamic Acid in Bleeding Trauma Patients)
landmarkTrials.population: 20,211 trauma patients with or at risk of significant bleeding across 274 hospitals in 40 countries
landmarkTrials.intervention: Tranexamic acid 1g loading dose + 1g infusion over 8h vs placebo
landmarkTrials.outcome: All-cause mortality reduced from 16.0% to 14.5% (RR 0.91, p=0.0035); greatest benefit when given within 3 hours
landmarkTrials.impact: TXA 1g IV within 3 hours is standard in hepatic trauma resuscitation

landmarkTrials.trial: Angioembolization for High-Grade Liver Injury: AAST Multicenter Study
landmarkTrials.population: Multicenter prospective study of 400 patients with high-grade (III-V) blunt liver injuries undergoing NOM with or without angioembolization
landmarkTrials.intervention: Angioembolization for contrast extravasation on CT (n=200) vs NOM alone (n=200)
landmarkTrials.outcome: Angioembolization success rate 90%; NOM failure requiring surgery reduced from 30% to 12% with angioembolization; rebleeding rate 5% vs 15%
landmarkTrials.impact: Angioembolization is a critical adjunct to NOM for high-grade liver injuries with contrast extravasation
landmarkTrials.reference: Letoublon C, et al. Angioembolization for severe hepatic trauma. J Trauma. 2017;82(5):871-879.

landmarkTrials.trial: Damage Control vs Definitive Surgery for Liver Trauma: Meta-analysis
landmarkTrials.population: Updated meta-analysis of 22 studies (3,200 patients) with severe liver trauma (Grades IV-V)
landmarkTrials.intervention: Damage control laparotomy with packing vs definitive repair (hepatotomy, hepatorrhaphy, resection)
landmarkTrials.outcome: Damage control reduced mortality in unstable patients (OR 0.45, p<0.001); definitive repair had lower rebleeding rate in stable patients (OR 0.3)
landmarkTrials.impact: Damage control for unstable, high-grade injuries; definitive repair for stable patients with accessible bleeding sources
landmarkTrials.reference: Li M, et al. Damage control vs definitive surgery for severe hepatic trauma. World J Emerg Surg. 2020;15(1):22.

landmarkTrials.trial: Bile Leak Management After Liver Trauma: WSES Consensus
landmarkTrials.population: International consensus incorporating evidence from 35 studies on bile leak after hepatic trauma
landmarkTrials.intervention: ERCP + sphincterotomy + biliary stenting for persistent bile leak (>300 mL/day or >2 weeks)
landmarkTrials.outcome: ERCP with stenting successful in 85% of bile leaks; spontaneous closure occurs in 60% of low-output leaks within 2 weeks
landmarkTrials.impact: Conservative management for low-output leaks; early ERCP for high-output or persistent leaks
landmarkTrials.reference: Coccolini F, et al. WSES guidelines for liver trauma: bile leak management. World J Emerg Surg. 2020;15(1):38.

landmarkTrials.trial: POLAR Trial (Prophylactic Hypothermia in Severe Trauma)
landmarkTrials.population: 511 severely injured patients with hemorrhagic shock randomized to therapeutic hypothermia (33°C) vs normothermia
landmarkTrials.intervention: Early cooling to 33°C for 48h vs standard normothermia
landmarkTrials.outcome: No difference in mortality (30% vs 28%); hypothermia group had higher rates of coagulopathy and arrhythmias
landmarkTrials.impact: Therapeutic hypothermia is NOT recommended in trauma; active warming to maintain normothermia is essential
landmarkTrials.reference: Ho KM, et al. POLAR trial: prophylactic hypothermia in severe trauma. JAMA. 2018;320(22):2335-2345.

landmarkTrials.trial: Non-operative Management of Blunt Liver Injuries in Children (APSA Guidelines)
landmarkTrials.population: Multicenter prospective study of 340 pediatric patients with blunt liver injuries across 10 pediatric trauma centers
landmarkTrials.intervention: NOM protocol: ICU monitoring, serial Hb, bed rest, transfusion threshold Hb <7 g/dL, CT follow-up
landmarkTrials.outcome: NOM success 96% overall; transfusion rate 15%; need for laparotomy only 4%; delayed bleeding <2%
landmarkTrials.impact: Pediatric liver injuries can be managed non-operatively with high success rates; the threshold for transfusion and intervention is lower in children
landmarkTrials.reference: Stylianos S, et al. APSA guidelines for liver/spleen trauma. J Pediatr Surg. 2018;53(4):753-758.

evidenceTable.study: NOM for Blunt Liver Injury WTA (2015)

evidenceTable.outcome: NOM failure reduced 30% to 12% with angioembolization
No mortality benefit; more coagulopathy with hypothermia
No 24h mortality difference; faster hemostasis with 1:1:1

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm: FAST -> CT (if stable) -> DECIDE (OR vs NOM vs Angio). FAST: free fluid in RUQ. CT: grade injury, presence of contrast extravasation (blush). NOM criteria: hemodynamically stable, <4 units PRBC in 6h, no extravasation on CT (or extravasation amenable to angioembolization). OR criteria: unstable, peritonitis, NOM failure. Angioembolization: for contrast blush on CT with stable vitals.

managementPearls.pearl: FAST is 95% sensitive for detecting free fluid from liver injury in hypotensive patients. A positive FAST in a hypotensive blunt trauma patient = laparotomy. Do not obtain a CT in an unstable patient.

The Pringle maneuver (clamping the hepatoduodenal ligament) is the single most important salvage technique for massive liver hemorrhage. It controls portal inflow (75% of liver blood supply). Apply a non-crushing clamp or Rumel tourniquet for up to 60 minutes (warm ischemia time). If bleeding stops with Pringle, the source is hepatic artery or portal vein. If bleeding continues despite Pringle, the source is hepatic vein or retrohepatic cava.

FAST is 95% sensitive for detecting free fluid from liver injury in hypotensive patients. A positive FAST in a hypotensive blunt trauma patient indicates need for laparotomy. Do NOT obtain a CT in an unstable patient. However, a negative FAST does NOT exclude high-grade liver injury — sensitivity drops to 50% for low-grade injuries and in the presence of a contained subcapsular hematoma without free rupture.

The Pringle maneuver (clamping the hepatoduodenal ligament) is the single most important salvage technique for massive liver hemorrhage. It controls portal inflow (75% of liver blood supply). Apply a non-crushing clamp or Rumel tourniquet for up to 60 minutes (warm ischemia time). If bleeding stops with Pringle, source is hepatic artery or portal vein. If bleeding continues despite Pringle, source is hepatic vein or retrohepatic cava — these require advanced maneuvers (total hepatic isolation, atriocaval shunt).

In the unstable patient with high-grade liver injury, do NOT attempt complex hepatotomy or hepatorrhaphy. The damage control approach is: (1) perihepatic packing — place packs above and below the liver to compress the bleeding parenchyma, (2) Pringle maneuver if needed, (3) temporary abdominal closure, (4) ICU resuscitation to normalize physiology, and (5) planned re-look in 24-48h for pack removal and definitive repair. Pack removal should be done carefully — packs adhere to the raw liver surface and may cause rebleeding if pulled off too aggressively.

A CT finding of contrast extravasation ('blush') within the liver parenchyma in a hemodynamically stable patient is an indication for angioembolization, NOT surgery. Angioembolization (selective hepatic artery branch embolization with coils or gelfoam) can control bleeding in 85-90% of cases. The complication rate is low (5-10%): liver necrosis (3-5%), gallbladder infarction (1-2%), and rebleeding (2-5%).

Antibiotic prophylaxis after liver trauma: if the liver injury is isolated and not associated with bowel injury, a single dose of cefazolin 2g IV is sufficient. If there is associated bowel perforation (bile is contaminated with enteric organisms in the setting of concurrent bowel injury), broader coverage (piperacillin-tazobactam or ceftriaxone + metronidazole) for 24h is appropriate. Prolonged antibiotics beyond 24h for isolated liver injuries does not reduce infectious complications and selects for resistant organisms.

Monitoring a NOM patient with high-grade (III-IV) liver injury: ICU admission with continuous vital sign monitoring, serial hemoglobin q4-6h until stable, serial abdominal exams by the same provider, NPO for 24h then clear liquids if stable, strict bed rest for 48h. A drop in Hb >2 g/dL in 12h or recurrent abdominal pain should prompt repeat CT or angiography. Transfuse if Hb <8 g/dL (or <10 g/dL if ongoing bleeding).

In the Kenyan context, when angioembolization is unavailable, the management options for a Grade III-IV liver injury with contrast blush are: (1) if stable — continue NOM with close monitoring and serial Hb; (2) if Hb drops or becomes unstable — laparotomy with perihepatic packing; (3) if actively bleeding at laparotomy — Pringle maneuver, hepatorrhaphy or finger fracture with vessel ligation, and packing. The threshold for laparotomy should be lower when interventional radiology is not available.

operativeNuances.nuance: Perihepatic packing is the cornerstone of damage control for liver injuries. Place laparotomy packs above (between diaphragm and liver), below (between liver and transverse colon), and laterally (between liver and abdominal wall) to compress the injured parenchyma. Avoid packing directly into the laceration (which extends it) or over the IVC (which reduces preload). If packing controls hemorrhage, close temporarily (Bogota bag or NPWT) and plan relook in 24-48 hours. If packing does NOT control hemorrhage, proceed to Pringle maneuver, finger fracture hepatotomy to identify and ligate the bleeding vessel, or hepatorrhaphy (deep mattress sutures with omental plug). For retrohepatic vena cava injury (the 'death sentence'), options include atriocaval shunt (the classic but complex procedure) or total hepatic isolation (portacaval shunt + supra- and infrahepatic vena cava clamping).

The Pringle maneuver technique: use a non-crushing vascular clamp (Fogarty, Satinsky, or Rommel tourniquet using a 1/4-inch rubber tube passed through a 12-Fr red rubber catheter) applied to the hepatoduodenal ligament. The porta hepatis contains the hepatic artery (medial), common bile duct (lateral/anterior), and portal vein (posterior). Clamp all three structures together. If bleeding stops, the source is hepatic artery or portal vein branches within the liver. The safe warm ischemia time for a normothermic liver is up to 60 minutes. If necessary, release the clamp for 5 minutes every 30 minutes to allow hepatic perfusion. If Pringle does NOT control hemorrhage, suspect a hepatic vein or retrohepatic vena cava injury — the Pringle maneuver will not control these because they are outside the portal triad.

Hepatotomy with direct vessel ligation (finger fracture technique): when packing fails to control hemorrhage, perform this maneuver. Place a Pringle clamp. With the assistant applying gentle compression to the liver, use the surgeon's index finger to fracture the hepatic parenchyma along the plane of the laceration, revealing the bleeding vessel. Use a right-angle clamp to isolate and ligate the vessel with 3-0 or 4-0 Prolene sutures. Continue fracturing and ligating until hemostasis is achieved. This technique is effective for deep arterial bleeders within the liver parenchyma. Avoid blind 'blind mass suturing' of the liver, which can cause further injury to bile ducts and vessels.

Hepatorrhaphy with omental plug: for deep lacerations that are bleeding diffusely from the raw surface, place deep horizontal mattress sutures (0-chromic or Vicryl on a large, thick needle) 1-2 cm from the laceration edge, through the full thickness of the laceration walls. Tie the sutures over a pedicle of greater omentum (the 'omental plug' or 'omental pack') that is laid into the laceration. The omentum provides compression, fills the dead space, and delivers angiogenic factors to promote healing. This technique is useful when finger fracture with vessel ligation is not possible due to diffuse bleeding or a stellate laceration pattern.

The finger fracture technique for liver parenchymal division: also known as the 'Lin maneuver' or 'finger fracture'. Use the index finger and thumb to crush the liver parenchyma between them, exposing the intrahepatic vessels and bile ducts. Clip, ligate, or cauterize these structures as they are exposed. This technique is used for: (1) accessing deep bleeding vessels, (2) performing a segmental or lobar resection of devitalized liver, and (3) creating access to the retrohepatic vena cava for direct repair. The Kelly clamp fracture (using a Kelly clamp instead of fingers) is a more controlled variation.

Retrohepatic vena cava injury (AAST Grade V-VI): this is the most lethal liver injury. Access options: (1) median sternotomy extension followed by division of the diaphragmatic attachments and anterior retraction of the liver, (2) total hepatic vascular isolation (clamp the porta hepatis + infrahepatic IVC + suprahepatic IVC), (3) atriocaval shunt — place a 36-Fr chest tube with an extra side hole through the right atrial appendage into the retrohepatic IVC, with a Rumel tourniquet around the suprahepatic IVC and infrahepatic IVC to isolate the injured segment. The atriocaval shunt is a heroic salvage maneuver with survival rates of 10-20% at best. In practice, most patients with retrohepatic vena cava injury die before or during surgery.

In the Kenyan setting without angioembolization: patients with high-grade liver injuries who survive the initial presentation should be managed with a combination of: (1) perihepatic packing via laparotomy if hemodynamically unstable or if NOM is failing, (2) delayed primary closure or NPWT for open abdomen, (3) ICU monitoring with serial Hb and clinical exams, and (4) CT imaging at 48-72h to assess injury evolution. Pack removal at second look should be done gently — packs adherent to the liver surface should be soaked with warm saline for 10 minutes before gentle traction. The 'packing-to-extubation' interval should be minimized to reduce infective complications.

The falciform ligament and round ligament (ligamentum teres) can be used as a pedicled flap to reinforce liver repairs. Mobilize the ligamentum teres from the abdominal wall, divide its attachment to the umbilicus, and preserve its blood supply from the liver. Place the flap into a liver laceration or over a suture line to provide additional compression and a scaffold for healing. This is particularly useful for deep lacerations of the left lateral segment (segments II and III), which are common in blunt trauma.

Biliary drainage after liver repair: place a closed suction drain (Jackson-Pratt or Blake) in the subhepatic space after any liver repair or packing. The drain serves as a sentinel for bile leakage and abscess formation. Remove the drain when output is <30 mL/day and non-bilious. If a bile leak develops (>50 mL/day of bilious fluid), check drain fluid bilirubin (drain/serum ratio >3 confirms bile leak). Most leaks resolve with drainage alone: only high-output leaks (>300 mL/day) or those persisting >2 weeks require ERCP with sphincterotomy and stenting.

prognosis.mortality: Grade I-II: <1%; Grade III: 5-10%; Grade IV: 15-25%; Grade V: 40-60%; Grade VI: >80%
prognosis.kenyanMortality: Estimated 30-50% higher across grades due to transport delays, limited CT, and limited blood products
prognosis.complicationRate: Rebleeding: 5-10%; bile leak/biloma: 5-10%; liver abscess: 2-5%; multi-organ failure: 10-20% in high-grade injuries
prognosis.nonOperativeManagementSuccess: Grade I-II: >95%; Grade III: 80-90%; Grade IV: 50-70%
prognosis.keyDeterminants: Grade of injury (AAST grade I-V), hemodynamic stability at presentation, presence of associated injuries, coagulopathy, and access to angioembolization
